medications.indication_rationale: Re-issue the patient's usual, historically effective NSAID that was stopped 48h ago; likely to control the flare. Take with food; check no renal/GI/CVD contraindication and maintain hydration.

medications.drug_name: IM ketorolac / injectable NSAID over chat
medications.indication_rationale: Injections cannot be given via telehealth; the oral NSAID restart addresses the flare.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Hand aids (jar/bottle openers, easy-open packaging) and self-care support during the flare.
Practical support (meals, medication) for an isolated patient with loss of hand function.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Wahls diet / high-dose vitamin D as therapy
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: No evidence for controlling an RA flare; not a substitute for NSAID/DMARD care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Restart meloxicam with breakfast and don't let it run out again — arrange repeats/help opening bottles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintaining drug levels controls the flare and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for a single fiery/red joint, red streaks up the arm, or a high fever, and seek urgent care if they occur.
Rest the hands, avoid tight gripping, and use easy-open cups/aids; arrange help for meals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Protects inflamed joints and maintains nutrition/hydration.

patient_education_points.point: Running out of your anti-inflammatory let your joints flare — restarting it should settle the swelling over the next day or so.

Because both hands are affected symmetrically with no single fiery joint, this looks like a flare rather than an infection — but we still want an in-person joint check and blood tests, and a rheumatologist to optimise your longer-term treatment.
Take the medication with food, rest your hands tonight, and get help opening bottles and preparing food while your grip is poor.
Go to ED / call 000 if one joint becomes fiery red and hugely swollen, red streaks travel up your arm, or you get a high fever with shaking chills.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within ~3 days for a joint check and rheumatology referral; sooner if worsening
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Joint examination, inflammatory markers/serology, NSAID tolerability, DMARD planning, self-care support
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A single fiery joint, tracking erythema, or high fever/rigors — seek urgent care.

must_include_items: Re-issue the usual NSAID (with cautions) that was stopped
Screen and safety-net septic arthritis/cellulitis (single hot joint, red streaks, high fever)
Arrange in-person joint review and rheumatology referral
Address practical self-care needs (opening medication, meals)
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day review if unable to self-care
errors_of_commission: Re-issuing the NSAID with no safety-netting
Presenting diet/vitamin D as a cure
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) restarting the usual NSAID with cautions, (2) septic-arthritis/cellulitis safety-netting, and (3) an in-person joint review/rheumatology pathway plus practical support. Just re-sending the script with no safety-netting is a fail.
